content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission


January 17, 1992




Honorable Robert Presley
State Senator, 36th District
State Capitol, Room 4048
Sacramento, CA  95814

                                          Re:  Your Request for Advice
                                               Our File No. A-91-553

Dear Senator Presley:

     This is in response to your letter requesting advice
regarding your responsibilities under the under the campaign and
conflict-of-interest disclosure provisions of the Political Reform
Act (the "Act").¹

                              QUESTION

     Are the turkeys you received from the Southern California
Water Company (hereinafter "Southern") in connection with a
"turkey giving project" a reportable campaign contribution or
gift as those terms are defined in the Act?

                              CONCLUSION

     The turkeys you received from Southern are reportable
campaign contributions.




___________
     ¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800  ●  P.O. Box 807  ●  Sacramento CA 95804-0807  ●  (916)322-5660

Our File No. A-91-553
Page 2


FACTS²

In early September, 1991, Senator Presley received a letter
from Southern concerning a turkey giveaway program to benefit the needy at Thanksgiving. Pursuant to this program, state senators
and assemblymembers selected by a group of private water companies donate to needy organizations of their choice 150 turkeys each.
Senator Presley was asked whether he would like to participate in the program. Senator Presley replied in the affirmative. A district staff person coordinated the arrangements with Southern.
He contacted and identified the local charities and nonprofit organizations to which the turkeys were to be given.

On Thanksgiving Day, November 26, 1991, Senator Presley's
Riverside District office received 150 turkeys from Southern.
District staff personnel, in turn, distributed these turkeys to the local charities and nonprofit organizations it had previously
selected. Subsequent thereto, the local charities or nonprofit
organizations, which had been the recipient of the turkeys, sent a
thank you letter to Senator Presley's Riverside District office
expressing their appreciation either for the Senator's "assistance
in providing" the turkeys or for his "donation" of the turkeys.

Southern notified your office that the issue of whether this
turkey giving program would result in the making of a contribution
or a gift to legislators under the Act had been cleared with the
Commission. Nevertheless, Senator Presley wanted independent verification.

ANALYSIS

The Act provides that the term "contribution" includes, in pertinent part, the distribution, transfer, loan, advance,
deposit, gift or other rendering of money, property, services or
anything else of value, whether tangible or intangible, for which
full and adequate consideration is not made, unless it is clear
from the surrounding circumstances that it is not made for
political purposes. (Section 82015.) Commission regulations


________________________________________

² The facts consist of the letter dated December 5, 1991, from Senator Presley requesting advice herein and attachments thereto
written by Catholic Charities, the Salvation Army, the Panel
Goeske Center, and the Casa Blanca Home of Neighborly Services; a letter to Senator Presley from the Southern California Water Company dated September 6, 1991; a telephone conversation on January 10, 1991, with John Harlan, a staff member from the Riverside District Office, and a letter to Senator Presley from
Southern dated September 6, 1991.

Our File No. A-91-553
Page 3


further clarify that a payment is made for political purposes if
it is received by or made at the behest of:

     A candidate, unless it is clear from
surrounding circumstances that the payment was
received or made at his or her behest for personal
purposes unrelated to his or her candidacy or
status as an office holder.

     Regulation 18215.

Applying the regulation to the present situation, it would appear
that the turkeys were given to you because of your status as an
officeholder.  Typically, when a candidate receives property,
services or anything else of value (other than a monetary payment)
for which the candidate has not provided full and adequate
consideration, the candidate has received an in-kind "nonmonetary"
contribution.

     The fair market value of the turkeys must, therefore, be
reported on your campaign statement as a nonmonetary contribution
from Southern.  Generally, the fair market value of goods or
services which are nonmonetary contributions is whatever it would
cost the candidate receiving the goods or services to purchase
them on the open market.

     Written advice was provided to the California Water
Association on November 18, 1991 concerning its program "Operation
Gobble," in which you participated.³  In that letter we set forth
the conditions that must exist in "Operation Gobble" so that the
water company's donation of the turkeys would not be considered a
contribution to the participating legislators.  Two of those four
conditions are missing in the present situation.  The turkeys were
given to the legislator, and the legislator was recognized as the
donor by some of the recipient local charities and nonprofit
organizations.


_______________________________

³ In the Adkisson Advice Letter, No. A-91-512, a copy of which is
attached hereto, the Commission had already concluded that the
"turkey giving project," entitled "Operation Gobble," in which
water companies would donate turkeys to various local charities
with the assistance of legislator, was not a gift under Section
82028.

Our File No. A-91-553
Page 4


     I trust this letter has answered your inquiry.  Please
contact me at (916) 322-5901 if I may be of additional assistance.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                        By:  Deanne Stone
                                             Counsel, Legal Division

SH:DS:aa

Enclosure

sections.question: Are the turkeys you received from the Southern California Water Company (hereinafter "Southern") in connection with a "turkey giving project" a reportable campaign contribution or gift as those terms are defined in the Act?
sections.conclusion:
The turkeys you received from Southern are reportable campaign contributions.

, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.
sections.facts:
In early September, 1991, Senator Presley received a letter from Southern concerning a turkey giveaway program to benefit the needy at Thanksgiving. Pursuant to this program, state senators and assemblymembers selected by a group of private water companies donate to needy organizations of their choice 150 turkeys each. Senator Presley was asked whether he would like to participate in the program. Senator Presley replied in the affirmative. A district staff person coordinated the arrangements with Southern. He contacted and identified the local charities and nonprofit organizations to which the turkeys were to be given.

On Thanksgiving Day, November 26, 1991, Senator Presley's Riverside District office received 150 turkeys from Southern. District staff personnel, in turn, distributed these turkeys to the local charities and nonprofit organizations it had previously selected. Subsequent thereto, the local charities or nonprofit organizations, which had been the recipient of the turkeys, sent a thank you letter to Senator Presley's Riverside District Office expressing their appreciation either for the Senator's "assistance in providing" the turkeys or for his "donation" of the turkeys.

Southern notified your office that the issue of whether this turkey giving program would result in the making of a contribution or a gift to legislators under the Act had been cleared with the Commission. Nevertheless, Senator Presley wanted independent verification.
sections.analysis:
The Act provides that the term "contribution" includes, in pertinent part, the distribution, transfer, loan, advance, deposit, gift or other rendering of money, property, services or anything else of value, whether tangible or intangible, for which full and adequate consideration is not made, unless it is clear from the surrounding circumstances that it is not made for political purposes. (Section 82015.) Commission regulations

2 The facts consist of the letter dated December 5, 1991, from Senator Presley requesting advice herein and attachments thereto written by Catholic Charities, the Salvation Army, the Janet Goeske Center, and the Casa Blanca Home of Neighborly Services; a letter to Senator Presley from the Southern California Water Company dated September 6, 1991; a telephone conversation on January 10, 1991, with John Harlan, a staff member from the Riverside District Office, and a letter to Senator Presley from Southern dated September 6, 1991.

further clarify that a payment is made for political purposes if it is received by or made at the behest of:

A candidate, unless it is clear from surrounding circumstances that the payment was received or made at his or her behest for personal purposes unrelated to his or her candidacy or status as an office holder.

Regulation 18215.

Applying the regulation to the present situation, it would appear that the turkeys were given to you because of your status as an officeholder. Typically, when a candidate receives property, services or anything else of value (other than a monetary payment) for which the candidate has not provided full and adequate consideration, the candidate has received an in-kind "nonmonetary" contribution.

The fair market value of the turkeys must, therefore, be reported on your campaign statement as a nonmonetary contribution from Southern. Generally, the fair market value of goods or services which are nonmonetary contributions is whatever it would cost the candidate receiving the goods or services to purchase them on the open market.

Written advice was provided to the California Water Association on November 18, 1991 concerning its program "Operation Gobble," in which you participated.3 In that letter we set forth the conditions that must exist in "Operation Gobble" so that the water company's donation of the turkeys would not be considered a contribution to the participating legislators. Two of those four conditions are missing in the present situation. The turkeys were given to the legislator, and the legislator was recognized as the donor by some of the recipient local charities and nonprofit organizations.

3 In the Adkisson Advice Letter, No. A-91-512, a copy of which is attached hereto, the Commission had already concluded that the "turkey giving project," entitled "Operation Gobble," in which water companies would donate turkeys to various local charities with the assistance of legislator, was not a gift under Section 82028.

I trust this letter has answered your inquiry. Please contact me at (916) 322-5901 if I may be of additional assistance.

CONCLUSION: The turkeys you received from Southern are reportable campaign contributions.
